sections.summary: Document title: 'Standard for Litchi (CXS 196-1995)'.
Records that the standard was adopted in 1995 and amended in 2005 and 2011.

This Standard applies to fresh litchis (Litchi chinensis Sonn.) of commercial cultivars supplied to the consumer after preparation and packaging and explicitly excludes litchis intended for industrial processing.

Sets the quality regime for litchis, including minimum requirements (e.g., whole, sound, clean, practically pest-free, free of abnormal moisture and foreign smell/taste, practically free of brown markings), maturity/transport conditions and colour variations, and classification into three classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with differing permitted defects and tolerances.

Lists minimum quality requirements for all classes: fruits must be whole, sound (no rot), clean, practically free of pests and pest damage, free of abnormal external moisture and foreign smell/taste, free of damage/abrasion and practically free of brown markings, and must be sufficiently developed and mature to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition, with specified colour variations including effects of sulphur dioxide fumigation.

sections.section: ## 2.1.1 The litchis must be sufficiently developed and mature.
sections.summary: Litchis must be sufficiently developed and mature to withstand transport and handling and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination, with colouring varying from pink to red for untreated fruit and from pale yellow to pink when fumigated with sulphur dioxide.

Defines three quality classes (Extra, Class I, Class II) with descending quality requirements: Extra requires superior, virtually defect-free fruit typical of the variety; Class I allows slight misshaping, colouring defects and skin defects up to 0.25 cm²; Class II accepts greater defects and skin blemishes up to 0.5 cm², provided minimum requirements are met.

'Extra' Class requires litchis of superior quality with typical shape, development and colouring and effectively free of defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance, quality, keeping quality or presentation.

Class I litchis must be of good quality and characteristic of the variety, but may show slight misshaping, slight colouring defects or slight skin defects provided total skin defect area does not exceed 0.25 cm² and appearance, quality, keeping quality and presentation are not affected.

Class II comprises litchis that meet the minimum requirements but not higher-class criteria and may have defects in shape or colouring and skin blemishes provided total blemish area does not exceed 0.5 cm² while retaining essential quality, keeping quality and presentation.

Size is measured by maximum equatorial diameter, with minimum sizes of 33 mm for 'Extra' Class and 20 mm for Classes I and II, and a permitted maximum size range of 10 mm between fruit within each package.

Specifies tolerances: quality tolerances (Extra: up to 5% by number/weight not meeting class but meeting Class I; Class I: up to 10% not meeting class but meeting Class II; Class II: up to 10% not meeting class or minimum except rotten/unfit produce) and size tolerances (up to 10% by number/weight not meeting minimum size provided diameter ≥15 mm and maximum size range of 10 mm is respected).

Quality tolerances by class: Extra allows 5% (by number/weight) of fruit not meeting its requirements but meeting Class I (exceptionally within its tolerances); Class I allows 10% not meeting its requirements but meeting Class II (or within its tolerances); Class II allows 10% not meeting class or minimum requirements except for fruit affected by rot or otherwise unfit.

Size tolerances allow up to 10% by number/weight of fruit in any class not meeting the minimum size provided their diameter is not less than 15 mm and the package does not exceed the permitted 10 mm maximum size range.

Presentation rules require package uniformity (same origin, variety, quality, size and colour), protective and clean/new (or food-grade recycled) packaging complying with CXC 44-1995 and container hygiene/ventilation/resistance standards, and permit presentation either individually (with pedicel cut at first knot and stalk ≤2 mm—mandatory for 'Extra') or in bunches (more than three attached litchis, branch ≤15 cm).
Each package must be uniform—containing only litchis of the same origin, variety/varietal type, quality, size and colour—and the visible contents must represent the whole package.

Packaging must protect the litchis, use new (or food-grade recycled), clean materials that do not damage the produce, allow printing/labels with non-toxic ink or glue, and comply with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport of Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 44-1995).

Containers must meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance requirements to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of litchis, and packages (or bulk lots) must be free of foreign matter and odours.

Litchis must be presented either individually (pedicel cut at first knot, stalk ≤2 mm beyond fruit—'Extra' Class mandatory) or in bunches (more than three attached well-formed litchis, branch ≤15 cm).

For individual presentation the pedicel must be cut at the first knot and the stalk may not extend more than 2 mm beyond the top of the fruit, and 'Extra' Class litchis must be presented individually.
For presentation in bunches the bunch must include more than three attached, well-formed litchis and the supporting branch must not exceed 15 cm in length.

Labelling requirements supplement the General Standard for Prepackaged Foods: consumer packages must show the produce name (and may show variety if not visible), and non‑retail containers or accompanying documents must indicate exporter/packer/dispatcher identification, nature of produce (and bunch specification if applicable), origin (country and optionally district/place), commercial identification (class, optional net weight) and may bear an official inspection mark.

Adds to the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) for consumer packages by requiring that when produce is not visible the package be labelled with the name of the produce and may include the variety or varietal type.
If the produce is not visible from the outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce and may be labelled with the variety or varietal type.

Non-retail containers must bear, legibly and indelibly on the same side or in accompanying documents (for bulk: on accompanying documents), specified particulars covering identification, nature, origin, commercial identification and an optional official inspection mark.
Requires name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher on non-retail containers; an identification code may be used optionally.
For non-retail containers, the name of the produce must be shown if contents are not visible; name of variety or commercial type is optional and a 'bunch' specification applies when relevant.
Non-retail containers must declare country of origin and may optionally indicate district or national/regional/local place name where grown.
Commercial identification for non-retail containers must indicate the class and may include net weight (net weight is optional).
Heading only indicating an optional official inspection mark for non-retail containers; no substantive detail is provided in this section.
Requires the produce covered by the Standard to comply with maximum contaminant/toxin levels in CXS 193-1995 and with Codex-established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

sections.section: 7.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).
sections.summary: Mandates compliance of the covered produce with the maximum levels set out in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).

sections.section: 7.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Mandates compliance of the covered produce with Codex Alimentarius Commission maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Recommends preparing and handling the produce in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.
